item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since the authorship is no longer clear from this: this skip was written by me, Tufto, on my first account prior to my deleting it. You can find more of my stuff here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *banging first on table* date the rock date the rock date the rock

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Surely the foundation would want to test breaking one of these rocks in half to see if separate personalities develop?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pleased to be on the upvote side of the borderline this time. Adding the pre-Earth element took what could have been a fairly well-worn premise in a newer direction, and while the final implications seem a little shoe-horned in, I really liked the easy flow of the dual interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone has clearly never seen Steven Universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lesbian talking rocks. Now those are some words I thought I wouldn't put together anytime soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a perfect description of what this article is. It starts off as quite silly, then gets profound. I upvoted.

I am sort of curious though, as to why this is euclid? Shouldn't it be safe? Is it because the Foundation doesn't understand it well enough?

item.title: Refreshing
item.content:encoded: In a wave of cliche ideas, this one is simple and refreshing. It reminds me of an easier time. +1 for originality and simplicity while still maintaining an air of mystery and sophistication.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This gives me such a fuzzy feeling. Good to see you've removed the clutter present in the draft and focused on the personalities of the rocks themselves. Also love how you've taken my suggestion about how they've seen time pass, and work it into the final log. Also, four interview logs is the perfect number of interview logs for this idea.

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You made talking rocks with British accents into a satisfying and thought provoking SCP. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow…the concept of talking rocks made me chuckle, but the interview logs were fantastic. Great work on those. The idea of the rocks existing somewhere before matter and existence is really interesting, and it took me a solid 15 minutes pondering that before I got back to writing this comment. Great overall, it leaves me wishing there was more.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really weird in a good way. I like the mix of stuff like punny names in a serious set of interviews. It sort of strikes an absurd yet existential tone. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't believe I upvoted talking rocks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not often that I get a chill from thinking about the implications of an SCP, but those last lines really hit me. Great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay you posted it! I saw it earlier today, and I have to say, I love it. The dialogue is really on point and completely makes the article. Unlike a lot of other articles, I was genuinely interested through every log. Nice job and +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although I liked this article from the moment the rocks started talking, it was definitely the concept of the rocks remembering all the way back to the beginning of the universe that made it worth it for me. Fantastic interview logs!

+1

This actually reminds me of a draft I attempted a while back, only it was living electrons instead of rocks. You've actually done really well with this idea, and the mention of ghosts in the context of the story doesn't feel too out of place. Nice job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With thanks to the following:

Varaxous
spudboy
MaliceAforethought
Updog
weryllium
